ppt.title: Life Processes - Session 1: Nutrition
ppt.presentationTitle: Understanding Life Processes: How Living Things Eat
ppt.presentationGoal: To teach students the fundamental concept of nutrition in living organisms, distinguishing between autotrophic and heterotrophic modes.

licensingNotes: Use Wikimedia Commons images by default and use in-app SVG/native slide text only as a fallback.
assetSearchPlan.fallbackStrategy: Use SVG diagrams with clear labels if specific Wikimedia assets are unavailable.

presentationWarnings: Do not introduce concepts from future sessions (e.g., detailed respiration or transport mechanisms).

learningOutcomesCovered: Differentiate between autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition

slides.teachingBeat: To introduce the topic and set the context for why living things need food.
mustTeach: Two main ways organisms get food
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Life Processes: Nutrition in Living Organisms
> Life Processes: Nutrition in Living Organisms
> Session 1 of Chapter: Life Processes
- Grade Level: Middle School Science
- What are Life Processes?
- Why do we eat?

<!-- layout: visual-first -->
<!-- purpose: title_identity -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: To introduce the topic and set the context for why living things need food.
Purpose: Introduce the chapter and session focus.
Explain: Start by asking students what they did today to get energy. Connect this to 'Life Processes'.
Ask: "What happens if we don't eat for a week?" Listen for answers like tiredness, hunger.
Board work: Write the chapter title and session number on the board.
Transition: Now let's define what these processes are.
Explain: Two main ways organisms get food
-->
slides.slideTitle: Life Processes: Nutrition in Living Organisms
slides.teacherIntent: To introduce the topic and set the context for why living things need food.
slides.studentTakeaway: Students will understand that 'eating' is a critical process for all life forms.

slides.visualPlan: Full-slide hero illustration of a lush green leaf with sun rays and water droplets on one side, transitioning to silhouettes of various animals (herbivore and carnivore) feeding in natural habitats on the other side. High contrast between bright greens/blues for plants and earthy tones for animals.

assets.altText: File:Drawing, Cow scratching her head, leaf plant, possibly 1844 (CH 18203879).jpg

Fotograf: Erik Olsen (1835 - 1920). Format: Fotopositiv. År: Ukjent (ca. 1900). Fotokopi fra en tidligere utstilling. Om fotografen: OLSEN, ERIK - Trondhjem 1869 - ca. 1916/17 f. i Meraker 2.5.1835 - d. i Trondhjem 16.10.1920. Arbeidet først på bergskriverkontoret i hjembygden, var så proviantskriver på Røros i 1863. Hadde deretter forskjellige kontoristjobber til han 1.6.1869 overtok Charles Foss's atelier på Bratøren i Trondhjem (Meisterlins Gaard) etter å ha gått i lære hos ham. I 1882 (muligens allerede i 1879) kjøpte han Søndre Gade 6 og flyttet atelieret dit i 1883. Fra 1902 var han i Olaf Trygvesøns Gade 21, og fra 1910 i Schultzgd. 7. Han var Domkirkens faste fotograf fra 1870 til ca. 1916/17. Endel av platene hans ble i 1895 av F. Scarlett laget til lysbilder til bruk for turistreklame, noen av dem ble farvelagt av den engelske kunstmaler Acres i London. Blant disse var noen fra Værdalsulykken. De gikk dessverre tapt på Norwegische Reisebureau i Berlin i 1944. Noen av hans plater ble (antakelig sammen med platene etter Charles Foss) overlatt til Trondhjems Historiske Forening. Platene og protokollene eies nu av Videnskabsselskabet, Museet. På Bergensutstillingen i 1898 stilte han ut "Fotografier, fra Visitkortformat til Legemsstørrelse". Han var formann i Nordenfjeldske Fotografers Forening ca. 1905/06, og ble æresmedlem der i 1913. Ivrig skiløper til langt opp i alderdommen. Hentet fra Bonge, Susanne: Eldre norske fotografer. Fotografer og amatørfotografer i Norge frem til 1920 (Universitetsbiblioteket i Bergen, 1980)

slides.teachingBeat: To provide a clear definition and list the key processes that keep organisms alive.
mustTeach: Essential functions performed by living beings
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Defining Life Processes
> Life Processes are essential activities.
> They keep us alive and functioning.
- - Nutrition (Getting Food)
- - Respiration (Breathing/Energy Release)
- Essential functions performed by living beings

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: core_concept -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: To provide a clear definition and list the key processes that keep organisms alive.
Purpose: Define the core concept of life processes.
Explain: Life is not just about being alive; it's about continuous activities. Without these, an organism dies.
Ask: "Can a machine have life processes?" Listen for 'No'. Explain that only living things do this automatically.
Correct: Clarify that while machines move or use energy, they don't grow, reproduce, or process food biologically.
Board work: List the 4 main processes we will cover in this chapter on the board.
Explain: - Nutrition (Getting Food)
-->
slides.teacherIntent: To provide a clear definition and list the key processes that keep organisms alive.
slides.studentTakeaway: Students can define life processes as essential activities for maintaining life.
bulletPoints: Essential functions performed by living beings
onSlideText: They keep us alive and functioning.
speakerNotes: Purpose: To provide a clear definition and list the key processes that keep organisms alive.
Purpose: Define the core concept of life processes.
Explain: Life is not just about being alive; it's about continuous activities. Without these, an organism dies.
Ask: "Can a machine have life processes?" Listen for 'No'. Explain that only living things do this automatically.
Correct: Clarify that while machines move or use energy, they don't grow, reproduce, or process food biologically.
Board work: List the 4 main processes we will cover in this chapter on the board.
Explain: - Nutrition (Getting Food)
slides.visualPlan: Clean diagram showing a human silhouette with icons pointing to different body parts representing each process: mouth (Nutrition), lungs (Respiration), heart/blood vessels (Transport), kidneys/bladder (Excretion).

assets.purpose: Visual aid for life processes overview
assets.altText: it is the image of human lungs and liver

animationHints: Appear icons one by one as each point is discussed
Highlight 'Nutrition' in green to transition to next topic

slides.teachingBeat: To explain the three main reasons why organisms need food.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# The Need for Food (Nutrition)
> Why do we need food?
> - To get ENERGY to move and work.
- - For REPAIR (healing wounds).
- 1. Energy for activities (Movement)
- 2. Growth of new cells

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: core_concept -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: To explain the three main reasons why organisms need food.
Purpose: Deepen understanding of the 'why' behind nutrition.
Explain: Use an analogy. A car needs fuel to run, but a human also uses food to build new muscle or heal a cut skin.
Ask: "Why do you need more food when you are growing fast?" Listen for answers about building body parts.
Board work: Draw a simple bar chart on the board showing 'Energy', 'Growth', and 'Repair' as equal pillars of nutrition.
Explain: - For REPAIR (healing wounds).
Explain: 1. Energy for activities (Movement)
-->
slides.teacherIntent: To explain the three main reasons why organisms need food.
slides.studentTakeaway: Students understand that food provides energy, raw materials for growth, and repair.
onSlideText: - To get ENERGY to move and work.
speakerNotes: Purpose: To explain the three main reasons why organisms need food.
Purpose: Deepen understanding of the 'why' behind nutrition.
Explain: Use an analogy. A car needs fuel to run, but a human also uses food to build new muscle or heal a cut skin.
Ask: "Why do you need more food when you are growing fast?" Listen for answers about building body parts.
Board work: Draw a simple bar chart on the board showing 'Energy', 'Growth', and 'Repair' as equal pillars of nutrition.
Explain: - For REPAIR (healing wounds).
Explain: 1. Energy for activities (Movement)
slides.visualPlan: Three distinct icons arranged horizontally: A running person (energy), a growing plant sprout or child height chart (growth), and a bandage on skin or bone repair symbol (repair).

assets.altText: Vein skeleton of a leaf. Veins contain lignin that make them harder to degrade for microorganisms. The leaf came from Magnolia doltsopa (Magnoliaceae).

animationHints: Fade in each icon as the corresponding point is explained.

slides.teachingBeat: To introduce and differentiate between the two main modes of obtaining food.
mustTeach: Autotrophic: Make own food from simple substances.
Heterotrophic: Depend on other organisms for food.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Two Modes of Nutrition
> How do organisms get food?
> 1. Autotrophs (Self-feeders)
- - Plants are the best example
- 2. Heterotrophs (Other-feeders)
- - Depend on plants or other animals

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: comparison -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: To introduce and differentiate between the two main modes of obtaining food.
Purpose: Introduce the classification of nutrition modes.
Explain: Break down the Greek roots if helpful. 'Auto' means self, 'Hetero' means different/other. 'Troph' means food/nourishment.
Ask: "Are humans autotrophs or heterotrophs?" Listen for 'Heterotrophs'. Ask why (we can't make our own food from sunlight).
Board work: Create a T-Chart on the board comparing Autotrophs and Heterotrophs with examples.
Transition: Let's look closer at how plants, the autotrophs, actually do this.
Explain: - Example: Plants, Green Algae
-->
slides.teacherIntent: To introduce and differentiate between the two main modes of obtaining food.
slides.studentTakeaway: Students can distinguish between organisms that make their own food (autotrophs) and those that depend on others (heterotrophs).
bulletPoints: - Depend on plants or other animals
Autotrophic: Make own food from simple substances.
speakerNotes: Purpose: To introduce and differentiate between the two main modes of obtaining food.
Purpose: Introduce the classification of nutrition modes.
Explain: Break down the Greek roots if helpful. 'Auto' means self, 'Hetero' means different/other. 'Troph' means food/nourishment.
Ask: "Are humans autotrophs or heterotrophs?" Listen for 'Heterotrophs'. Ask why (we can't make our own food from sunlight).
Board work: Create a T-Chart on the board comparing Autotrophs and Heterotrophs with examples.
Transition: Let's look closer at how plants, the autotrophs, actually do this.
Explain: - Example: Plants, Green Algae
slides.visualPlan: Split screen comparison. Left side: A bright green plant with a sun icon and water drop (Autotroph). Right side: A cow eating grass or a lion hunting (Heterotroph). Clear visual distinction between 'making' vs 'taking'.

assets.altText: A cow searching and grazing in grassy land with beautiful landscape.

animationHints: Animate the split screen to slide in from opposite sides

slides.teachingBeat: To provide a simplified but accurate overview of the photosynthetic process as an example of autotrophy.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# How Plants Make Food (Photosynthesis)
> Photosynthesis: The Plant's Kitchen
> Ingredients Needed:
- - Water (from roots)
- - Carbon Dioxide (from air)
- Result:

<!-- layout: board-example -->
<!-- purpose: worked_example -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: To provide a simplified but accurate overview of the photosynthetic process as an example of autotrophy.
Purpose: Explain the mechanism of autotrophic nutrition.
Explain: Use a cooking analogy. Sunlight is the stove, water and CO2 are ingredients, chlorophyll (in leaves) is the chef/kitchen equipment.
Ask: "What gas do we breathe out that plants need?" Listen for 'Carbon Dioxide'. What gas do they give us back?
Board work: Write a simple word equation on the board: Sunlight + Water + CO2 -> Food + Oxygen.
Transition: Now you know how autotrophs eat. Heterotrophs have different ways.
Explain: - Inputs: Sunlight + Water + Carbon Dioxide
-->
slides.teacherIntent: To provide a simplified but accurate overview of the photosynthetic process as an example of autotrophy.
slides.studentTakeaway: Students understand that plants use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to make food (glucose) and release oxygen.
speakerNotes: Purpose: To provide a simplified but accurate overview of the photosynthetic process as an example of autotrophy.
Purpose: Explain the mechanism of autotrophic nutrition.
Explain: Use a cooking analogy. Sunlight is the stove, water and CO2 are ingredients, chlorophyll (in leaves) is the chef/kitchen equipment.
Ask: "What gas do we breathe out that plants need?" Listen for 'Carbon Dioxide'. What gas do they give us back?
Board work: Write a simple word equation on the board: Sunlight + Water + CO2 -> Food + Oxygen.
Transition: Now you know how autotrophs eat. Heterotrophs have different ways.
Explain: - Inputs: Sunlight + Water + Carbon Dioxide
slides.visualPlan: A clear flowchart diagram showing a leaf with arrows pointing in (Sunlight icon, Water drop from soil, CO2 bubble) and an arrow pointing out (Glucose molecule symbol, Oxygen gas bubbles).
animationHints: Animate arrows to show flow from ingredients to product
Highlight 'Oxygen' in green as it's vital for life

slides.teachingBeat: To assess student understanding through quick questions.
mustTeach: 1. Name one life process we discussed today.
3. Is a mushroom an autotroph or heterotroph? Why?
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Check Your Understanding
> Quick Check!
- - What are the three main reasons for eating?
- - Which group makes its own food: Autotrophs or Heterotrophs?
- - Name one example of each.

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: quick_assessment -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: To assess student understanding through quick questions.
Purpose: Formative assessment.
Ask: Read questions aloud. Give students 30 seconds to think, then call on volunteers.
Listen for: 'Energy', 'Growth', 'Repair' as reasons. Correct any confusion between autotrophs/heterotrophs immediately.
Board work: Write correct answers on the board after discussion.
Explain: 1. Name one life process we discussed today.
Explain: 2. Why do organisms need food?
-->

slides.teachingBeat: To summarize key takeaways and bridge to the next session.
mustTeach: - Life processes keep us alive (Nutrition, Respiration...)
- Autotrophs make food; Heterotrophs eat others.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Summary: Life Processes Start with Food
> Key Takeaways:
> 1. Life Processes are essential for survival.
- 2. Nutrition is the first step: getting energy and materials.
- 3. Two modes: Autotrophic (Self) vs Heterotrophic (Other).
- Next Session: How do plants actually get water and CO2? (Detailed Photosynthesis)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: summary -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: To summarize key takeaways and bridge to the next session.
Purpose: Wrap up the session.
Explain: Recap the three main points. Emphasize that this is just the beginning of understanding how life works.
Transition: "Tomorrow we will dive deeper into exactly where plants get their ingredients and what happens inside a leaf.".
Explain: - Life processes keep us alive (Nutrition, Respiration...)
Explain: - Food provides Energy, Growth, Repair.
Explain: - Autotrophs make food; Heterotrophs eat others.
-->

slides.teachingBeat: To assign homework and set expectations for the next class.
mustTeach: - Observe a plant in your garden or school.
- Note: Where is it? What color are its leaves?
- Think: How does this plant get food without moving?
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Your Task for Tomorrow
> Homework:
> 1. Find a green leaf today.
- 2. Draw or describe where you found it.
- 3. Question for tomorrow: "How can a stationary object make its own food?"
- See you next time!

<!-- purpose: homework_next_session -->
